speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Andrea Brasg. I'm interviewing Henia Bryer. Today is the 15th of November, 1995. The interview is being conducted in Bloemfontein, South Africa. And the interview is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. So you were telling me about the Radom ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm trying to remember some more incidents. There is one that comes to mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the medical facilities there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were. There was a hospital. Jewish doctors were working in it. And they had very limited supplies. But whatever they could, they did. And that was that. I've never been, luckily, to this hospital, because they used to-- a lot of people died, obviously. There were not enough medicines and not enough supplies. But there was a kind of a hospital. Now, that reminds me of something that happened personally to me in the small-- in the ghetto. And I had an abscess on my tooth. And it was very painful. My whole face was swollen. And it was full of stuff there. And it had to be drained and cleaned. And my mother decided I have to go to the hospital to have it done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were going to the hospital the following morning. And she asked my uncle, who was still alive there, to come and help her with me because I had high temperature, ad my whole face was enormously swollen. And she asked him to come with her, with me, to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he arrived in the morning. And he was helping me to put my jersey on and a coat on because it was very cold outside. And he was clumsy. He wasn't used to dressing girls. And he hit me. I don't know what he did. But he hurt me here. And this whole thing burst open.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we had to clean it up and put hot compresses on. And it was a whole big business. But we missed the transport to the hospital. We couldn't go to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that very same morning, the whole hospital was taken away. The sick patients were shot dead. And everybody else was sent to camps. So it was-- I suppose it was a lucky break for me, because I never got there. If I would have gotten there, I think my mother and I would have been either shot or sent away that very day. So that was the first escape I had. Another incident in the ghetto, one day, they come-- the SS come and they announce, everyone who has family in Palestine must register. There is a train going to Italy. And from there, they going to join their relatives in Palestine. So there was great joy and jubilation among the people. And they still believed them. That was the funny thing. They thought, maybe, hoping. And they registered. And some wonderful families, whole families, came to register. We didn't. Our whole family was in-- was in Palestine. But we didn't. I don't know why my parents decided ah, we wait and see what's happening here. And this whole lot of people were taken out of town and shot dead, just like that, all of them. So that was-- that was the second escape we had, because we did really have family in Palestine, but we didn't say anything. I mean, my mother and father decided, nuh-uh, we're not going to go. And people were trying to get on the list, trying to bribe officials, and paying fortunes to get on that list. And they were all killed, all of them. So this was-- this was the ghetto experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have contact with the outside world? Radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little at that stage. When I was in that place where I worked, in the-- we did have contact because the Polish people used to come in. And they were telling us what's happening in the world. But they were very vague about what's happening to the Jews. They-- they didn't know. We knew that they were sent to concentration camps. We knew that they were being sent somewhere else. But we never imagined in our wildest dreams that they were systematically gassed and killed like they were. We were told that they were going to another work camp. That's what the Germans used to tell them. But that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever try to escape, or friends to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have contacts outside the ghetto. And we didn't-- we didn't have any contacts. You had to have false papers and contacts outside the ghettos. And we were too young to be on our own, to just escape and fend for ourselves. And my parents didn't have a chance. The couldn't leave the children. And a whole family to hide was very difficult. Quite a number of young people, but older than I, did escape. And they lived in the Polish quarter. Some of them survived. Some didn't. A lot were caught and shot. But at least, they tried to escape. We didn't We stayed until the bitter end. And the last small ghetto was liquidated in March 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then were did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came-- always, everything happened at the crack of dawn. It was still pitch dark. It was cold. There was snow outside. Polish winters were very harsh in those days. I believe the climate has changed now. But in those days, it was very cold. It was covered with snow and ice. And it was pitch dark when they came to round us up. And there were not too many left. I'm not sure about the numbers. But I have an idea about 300 more or less. I'm not sure. We were-- still had our belongings with us. Each one was allowed one suitcase. So we each had a suitcase with us and all our personal stuff like watches, rings, the bit of jewelry that remained that wasn't sold. I had a watch, a gold watch. I had a ring that I got, 12th birthday, I think. And I had a chain which my grandmother gave me. And I didn't want to part with it. So I was wearing it all the time. And we loaded onto these cattle trucks. They were locked. And on the trip to Majdanek. Now, Majdanek was just outside Lublin. And luckily, it wasn't all that far away. I can't remember how long that journey took. It was a nightmare because it was our first experience of being herded into these cattle trucks. There was no food given on the journey, as far as I remember, no water. And we arrived in Majdanek. And that was that. And then they got us out of the trucks. You had to jump. These trucks were very high. There was no ladders, no steps to help us. We had to just get off it. And literally herded to the camp. And then we were separated. The men were separated from the women. And we were taken to a bath house or whatever they called it. Cold water, the middle of winter, and you're just stripped, leave all your belongings. And you never saw them again. And you were given, when you came out it shivering. And that was the first time I saw the SS women that were-- they were Nazi guards, the women. They were very, very cruel, all of them. And the one that was in charge of them was particularly-- I remember her as if it was yesterday. Her name was Elsa Erich. She had eyes like glass, steel. And she always smiled when she hit somebody, a real sadist. They were wearing riding boots and uniforms with a SS insignia, like the men. And they were in charge of the women. They herded us into this bath house. Cold water, as I said before. And everything that we possessed was left outside. There was nothing. We were just like that. And then we were given these striped dresses and clothes to wear, underwear and whatever. It was all taken from other prisoners that were killed before us. And it was laundered and given to us. I can't remember delousing. Maybe there was. But we still came from home, so we were pretty clean at that stage. But they did spray us with some awful stuff and gave us these terrible uniform. And they gave us canvas shoes with wooden soles. Now, that was a disaster because the snow used to stick to it and you couldn't walk. So it was murder. But that was it. And we were taken to a barrack, all of us, all the women and children. There were still children. My younger sister was there. And some other women with children were there. And the barracks were not too bad. There were wooden-- sort of-- I wouldn't call them beds. They were planks. But it was reasonably clean. And we had like straw mattresses. And we were given a blanket as well. And there to meet us came a young woman who was in charge. She was what they called the block elder there. She was in charge of that block. And we met some other women. Now, I just want to describe to you Majdanek. At that stage, Majdanek was one of the extermination camps. It was divided into five fields. And the crematorium was in the fifth one. So whenever you heard that somebody was taken to the fifth field, you knew he's never going to come back from there. I can't remember which number we were, which field. But when we arrived in Majdanek, there were very few Jewish people there. They've killed most of them. They were the men from our transport, from Radom. There were the group of girls that I'll talk about in a minute, that were left from the Lodz ghetto. Because when they closed the ghetto in Lodz, it was one of the biggest ghettos in Poland. According to the statistics, there were 200,000 Jews in the Lodz ghetto. And most of them were taken to Majdanek when they liquidated the ghetto. And most of them were killed there. But out of all these multitudes of people, they selected 100 young women. And they were beautiful. Each one of them was prettier than the other. And we looked at them. And couldn't understand what was going on there. They were well-dressed. And they seemed to be well fed. And they were beautiful, each one and one. And eventually, we found out what was the story with these girls. First, they were working in the stores where they collected all the jewelry and money and all the things that the people brought with them. And there were masses of stuff there, clothes and jewelry and gold and masses of stuff. So they were working with this. And then, they were working in a German brothel. They used them for the soldiers who came from the Eastern Front. That was their work. And they were wonderful to us. They helped us a lot. We didn't come in contact with all 100. But we knew there were a hundred of them. They brought us extra food whenever they could. And they were very good. They really helped us a lot. But the conditions in the camp and what was going on there was just something horrific. It was our first introduction to a concentration camp. And for the first time, we realized that people are being exterminated. There is a machine. There is a factory that kills people. This was the first time we came in contact with all these horrible things. There were a lot of the Russian civilian women there from the villages, very simple women. And their lot was very miserable. They were treated very badly. They were beaten day and night. And they didn't know what's happening. They couldn't understand a word of German. We all spoke German. So we knew, at least, what they wanted, as is impossible as it was, the tasks-- the tasks that we were given. But we knew what-- we could communicate with them. But they didn't understand a word of German. They couldn't speak Polish. And they were so miserable. They cried all the time. Whenever we came in touch with them, they were crying. They were terrible. There were a lot of the Russian prisoners of war as well, Russian men. We only saw them through the wire because we were separated from the men. But we didn't encounter any other people, Jewish people, at that stage. And these women told us that they exterminated there thousands of people. According to the statistics, in Majdanek, 750,000 people were killed in this crematorium there. So it's a horrible story. Now, we were put to work. And it was total chaos. The first work, I remember, they brought us into a big, big, tremendous soccer hall. But it was outside. It only had a roof. It didn't have walls or anything like that. And it was cold. And they told us to sort out millions of shoes. There were piles of it. It was impossible to sort them out. And while you were trying to do that, they screamed. And they beat us. And they shouted. And it was an impossible task. We tried to do the best we could. So that was the first few days of our introduction to this first concentration camp. And then I was chosen for another job. They had a basket weaving workshop there. And we had to make baskets for the bombs. They were like a cylinder. Each basket was the size of a bomb, I suppose. It was a cylinder that big. And we had to weave these things with reeds. And the conditions were also terrible. And we were divided. The Germans divided everything into 100 and 10, in groups of tens and groups of hundreds like the ancient Romans. And so I was in a group of 10. And we had to produce, each of us, eight baskets a day, which under normal circumstances, I suppose is not too bad. But in this freezing cold with the fingers not being so supple and the reeds had to be soaked in cold water, it was very difficult. But we had to do it. Otherwise, there were punishments and all sorts of things. And but I must say, we did help each other. Now, the older people couldn't-- just couldn't do all that much. So whatever extra we could do, we helped each other so that the quota should be done. And this was the work that I did while-- while I was in Majdanek, all the time there. We were only there for six weeks. It was long enough, believe me. But six weeks because they were folding up the camps while the Russians were advancing. It was already Spring, 1945, March. And they were-- the crematorium, we could see that there was no smoke coming anymore from there. And they sent-- before they sent us away, they sent the 100 young women away. And they came to say goodbye. And they were so happy because they reasoned that if they wanted to kill them, they would have killed them here and there. They have the facilities. So if they're sending them away, it means it's a good sign, they sending them to another concentration camp. But it wasn't a good sign. They sent them to Auschwitz. And there, all 100 went straight to the gas chambers. We found that out a few days later when we arrived in Auschwitz, because there were people who worked there. And they told us, oh, those beauties from Majdanek, they were all sent to the gas chambers. So they were happy for no reason at all. They-- they didn't survive. And this is this very, very sad story. And we were sent-- from Majdanek, we were sent to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Majdanek, my mom, my dad, and my younger sister. Now, they were sent to Auschwitz straight away, those 100 girls. And we were sent to Plaszow. When we came later to Auschwitz, we were told about what happened to them. But we were-- in the meantime, we were sent to Plaszow. And we were still four of us, my mother, my little sister, my father, and I. And there we arrived six weeks later. That was March, probably the end of April. And it was a big, big camp. And it was very well depicted in this film, But I think it was a bit toned down still. There was a Jewish couple whom the Germans appointed to be in charge of the prisoners. They were walking around there and giving orders. Besides that were the SS men, the guards, the main guards. The commandant was the cruelest man I've ever met. And the SS women, the aufseherins, they called them, which means supervisor. But they were not really supervisors. They were soldiers. They were wearing uniforms. And the woman that was in charge was the same woman that was in Majdanek. They all were transferred to Plaszow. At the same time as we came, they came as well, these women SS. And it was-- most of the people there we didn't know because they were people from Krakow, most of them. And we were like foreigners there. We came from a different town. We didn't know many people. And they put us to work. All of them-- all of us were put to work. Obviously, when you come in, it's the usual routine, the selection, the feeble and elderly to the left, the ones who remain alive to the right, stripped again of everything and given other striped uniforms. By that time, we didn't have any possessions. We didn't have anything. We just changed into other striped uniforms. And we were sent into barracks. They were more crowded. They were-- They were not as comfortable. The rations were meager. But there were still some rations. And we were put to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sanitary facilities like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were places-- no hot water, no luxury at all, but you could wash. And they had these primitive latrines, hundreds of rows of these wooden things and so on. Very primitive, but they were still there. And it was difficult. But if you're used to it, you kept yourself clean as much as you could obviously. And we were sent to work in Plaszow. But there were lots of beatings there and lots of hangings there. Every morning, you used to see people hanging from ropes. And the worst of all these places was the standing on a Pilplatz. In every camp, in every concentration camp, there was a big square where they used to count us every morning. So now, we were wakened about 5 o'clock, sometimes 4 o'clock in the morning, by the Jewish supervisors. And we had to stand there outside in rows for hours because the Germans only came about 8 o'clock or whenever. And we had to stand there for hours outside waiting for them. And then they used to count everybody. And they used to send us to work in groups of 10 again. At the beginning, when I was in Plaszow, they used to give us back breaking work. There was a quarry somewhere nearby. And we had to push trolleys full of stones, sometimes uphill, sometimes straight. We never knew where these trolleys were going and what it was for. But we had to push them. And it was very difficult. It was hard work. It wasn't for women. It wasn't for young people, for young girls. It wasn't for children. But we have to do it. And we did that for quite a few weeks. And then things-- a lot of things happened all of a sudden, after a few weeks when we were in Plaszow. And also, they had another thing that you had to strip and walk naked in the camp so that they can do these awful selections. Every couple of weeks. It was the most dreadful thing. You can imagine how you feel when you're asked to strip. And I mean, we were brought up in a Jewish home. I wouldn't even strip for my mother in the bathroom. Used to lock the door when I went to have my bath or things like that. And they had to strip naked, whether it was summer or winter, and to walk in the camp or all these Germans to look. They were walking around with riding crops and making remarks, ugly remarks, while we were doing it. So it was pretty awful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they make the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whoever they didn't like. If you looked too thin or too-- well, there were no fat people there at all. But if you looked old to them, and a woman of 40 could look old in those days when she was starved and a long time in the camp. We were still lucky that we came to the camps late in the war. But there were people who where there since 1942, '43. So obviously, by that time, they were skeletons. They were thin. They were tired. They couldn't work anymore. So they had them killed. And the trouble was that there was no crematorium in Plaszow. They used to shoot everybody or hang everybody and then burn their bodies on a hill nearby. And you had this constant smell wifting into the camp and the ashes in the air. All the time, everything was gray. And we knew. I mean, we could see it. It was the fires were burning day and night. It was dreadful. Then after a while, I suppose I had a lucky break then. We are standing on the Pilplatz, on this big square being counted. And this SS woman that was in charge, this Elsa Erich, chose me to be their maid. She asked me to come out. And she looked me over like I was a piece of merchandise. And she said I must report now and work as a maid for the SS women. But before that happened, I lost my family. My mother was sent to another camp from Plaszow a few weeks after we arrived there. My father was killed by the guards. He was just beaten to death a few weeks after we arrived there. And my little sister was still there. But she was kept in a separate block with all the children. And I couldn't see her. I could see her perhaps once a week on a Sunday, when we didn't work. Then I could go and see here there. But otherwise, they were isolated. All the children were in a special block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the Jewish couple that was in charge of you, were they cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were. They were. They were. They were not nice people. But they chose-- you know, whoever would do a job like that wasn't a nice person because I had a cousin in the ghetto, a young man, who was chosen to be a policeman in the ghetto. And he did go because, you know, it was something to do. And as I said, once you had a job, you-- you were safe for the time being. You weren't sent away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he worked as a policeman. But he was a very, very nice, kind young man. And the first time they asked him to go and arrest Jews, he said, don't be silly. I'm not going to do that. So they said, you have to. This is your job. And he said, no way am I going to do that. Here is your hat. Here is your band. And I'm finished with the job. And he was sent out with the next transport and killed.
